era_context: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor is at the height of its power and cultural achievement. The Forbidden City is being completed in Beijing, which has recently become the capital. The Hussite Wars are in their sixth year following Jan Hus's execution in 1415. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean. The Renaissance is beginning to emerge in Italy. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire is consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas beyond Aztec and Inca heartlands

events.title: Forbidden City completed; Beijing becomes Ming capital
events.description: The Forbidden City, the imperial palace complex in Beijing, was completed in 1420 after nearly two decades of construction. The completion of the Forbidden City marked the consolidation of Beijing as the capital of the Ming Dynasty, replacing Nanjing. The construction of the Forbidden City was one of the greatest architectural projects of the medieval world, requiring the labor of hundreds of thousands of workers and vast resources. The complex served as the residence of the emperor and the seat of imperial government, and its construction symbolized Ming imperial power and legitimacy. The Forbidden City remains one of the most impressive architectural achievements of the medieval world and a testament to Ming engineering and artistic skill.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The completion of the Forbidden City in 1420 is well-documented in Ming records, and the surviving structure provides archaeological confirmation of its construction date.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; Ming maritime dominance
events.description: In 1420, Zheng He's treasure voyages were in their second decade, continuing to project Ming power and prestige across the Indian Ocean. The voyages brought back exotic goods, tribute from foreign rulers, and diplomatic recognition of Ming supremacy. Zheng He's fleets visited ports in East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Southeast Asia, establishing trade networks and collecting tribute. These voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime exploration and were supported by the Yongle Emperor, who viewed them as demonstrations of imperial power and legitimacy. The voyages were economically costly and were eventually discontinued after the Yongle Emperor's death in 1424.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan's Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Zheng He's voyages in 1420 is confirmed by Ming records, though the exact itineraries and dates of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Jan Žižka's military campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: In 1420, Jan Žižka continued his military campaigns in Bohemia, consolidating Hussite control and defending against Catholic crusades. Žižka's innovative tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg), made Hussite armies formidable opponents. In 1420, Žižka was engaged in campaigns against both Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. His military successes strengthened the Hussite position in Bohemia and made him a legendary figure in Czech history. The Hussite Wars represented one of the earliest challenges to papal authority and foreshadowed the later Protestant Reformation of the 16th century.
events.certainty_note: Jan Žižka's military campaigns in 1420 are documented in contemporary Hussite chronicles and later historiographic accounts.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power under Murad II
events.description: In 1420, the Ottoman Empire was consolidating its power in Anatolia and expanding into the Balkans. Murad II, who would ascend to the throne in 1421, was already a major figure in Ottoman politics. The Ottomans had recently defeated various rival powers and were establishing themselves as the dominant force in the eastern Mediterranean. The empire was on the cusp of becoming a major Mediterranean and European power, though it would not reach its full territorial extent until the reign of Suleiman the Magnificent in the 16th century. Ottoman expansion in this period was gradual but relentless, driven by military prowess and administrative efficiency.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in this period is well-documented in Ottoman chronicles and European sources.

events.title: Early Italian Renaissance: Humanist scholars and artists active
events.description: In 1420, the Italian Renaissance was in its early stages, with humanist scholars and artists beginning to revive classical learning and artistic techniques. Florence was emerging as a center of cultural innovation, with figures like Cosimo de' Medici beginning to patronize artists and scholars. The rediscovery of classical texts and the study of ancient Greek and Roman civilization were reshaping European intellectual life. In 1420, artists like Masaccio (born 1401) were beginning their careers, introducing new techniques of perspective and naturalism into painting. The Renaissance represented a gradual shift from medieval to early modern European culture, with roots extending back to the 13th and 14th centuries.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (Florentine banker and patron)

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (later compilation)
Surviving artworks and manuscripts from the period

events.certainty_note: The early Renaissance in Italy is well-documented through surviving artworks, manuscripts, and later biographical accounts. The dating of specific cultural developments is sometimes approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1420 as a turning point in world history is debatable. While the completion of the Forbidden City was a major architectural achievement, some scholars argue that it did not represent a fundamental shift in Ming power or prestige, as the empire had already been dominant for decades. The dating of the Forbidden City's completion is sometimes given as 1420 or 1421, with some sources suggesting the completion was gradual. The Hussite Wars are sometimes portrayed as a major challenge to papal authority, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Hussite movement was ultimately suppressed and that the major religious upheaval came later with the Protestant Reformation of the 16th century. The Italian Renaissance is often portrayed as a sudden cultural flowering, but modern scholarship emphasizes the gradual continuity between medieval and Renaissance culture, with roots extending back to the 13th and 14th centuries. Ottoman expansion in 1420 was a gradual process, and the year 1420 does not represent a clear turning point.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1420 is dominated by East Asian and European sources, with rich documentation of political, military, and cultural events. The Ming Dynasty is well-documented in court records and later historiographic accounts, though the perspective is heavily weighted toward elite male actors and state-level politics. The completion of the Forbidden City is confirmed by both documentary and archaeological evidence. The Hussite Wars are documented in contemporary chronicles, but the perspective is often biased toward either the Hussite or Catholic side. The Italian Renaissance is known through surviving artworks and later biographical accounts, which may emphasize certain figures and developments over others. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Pacific Islands, and interior Americas remain virtually absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by elites and may not reflect the experiences of common people or marginalized groups.

graph_edges.note: The completion of the Forbidden City in 1420 was part of the Yongle Emperor's broader program of imperial consolidation that began after his ascension in 1402.
Zheng He's voyages in 1420 were part of the larger series of treasure voyages that began in 1405.
The Hussite Wars in 1420 were a direct result of Jan Hus's execution in 1415.
Ottoman consolidation in 1420 was a precursor to the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
The early Renaissance cultural developments in 1420 contributed to the flourishing of Italian art and humanism in subsequent decades.
